payload.text:
I want to build an app/website that allows food vendors to draw/sketch their stand. 

This should be an extremely intuitive and simple tool for the traders to use.

The first page should be a sign in page that asks for the Vendor-ID and Trader Name.

The second page should be the working app:

First, a boundary size should be created (e.g. 10m by 8m)

 I want this to be a button based system; the trader selects an object (e.g. pergoda, food truck or smoker) from a menu on the left, an object icon (to scale) will appear into the stand boundary.
The list of selectable objects should be:
Pergoda (make this translucent and ignore the collision warning so that the user can place items within the object)
Van/Truck (make this translucent and ignore the collision warning so that the user can place items within the object)
Smoker
Hot Hold
Oven
Fridge
Freezer
Baine Marie
Asado
Fryer
Other (Hot)
Other (aesthetic)
Other (cold)
Other (misc)
Pitmaster
Chef
Manager
Server

The object icon should be scalable from an input box that appears when the object is selected by the cursor. There should be two editable fields (width and length). The object should also be size adjusted by dragging the corner (like an image in a word document).

Make sure multiple objects can be placed within the boundary, but if they are touching or go outside the boundary a warning sign should appear (apart from pergoda and van/food truck).

This then needs to be exported as a clean image/pdf

**Initiating Stand Design**

I'm starting to visualize this React application for food vendors. Right now, I'm focusing on the initial structure: a simple login page with vendor ID and trader name fields. From there, I'll move to the stand designer, allowing vendors to define boundary sizes – let's say 10m x 8m initially. I need to make sure the interface is intuitive and easy to use.


**Defining Stand Components**

I've fleshed out the sidebar with draggable objects like Pergodas, Vans, and various cooking appliances. Each has specific interaction methods. Size adjustment will be handled via input boxes or corner dragging with constraints to avoid boundary breaches, with exceptions for the Pergoda and Van. I'm aiming for clean image or PDF exports. I'll need a way to track the state of the canvas, which React Hooks might solve.



**Refining Visual Feedback**

I'm now integrating collision detection and visual cues. I'm focusing on a grid system for alignment and smooth transitions during item placement. Overlays and red borders will indicate collisions or boundary breaches. I'm also considering the best method for implementing drag-and-drop, possibly using a library to simplify the process. Default sizes for selectable items are being added to the data structures.

payload.text:
Add the ability to rotate placed objects within the food stand boundary. Users should be able to select an object and use a control (e.g., a rotation handle or keyboard input) to adjust its orientation.

Implement undo and redo functionality for the food stand drawing tool. The user should be able to reverse or reapply their last actions, such as placing, scaling, or moving objects.

**Considering New Features**

I'm currently focused on incorporating object rotation into "VendorSketch Pro". The plan involves allowing users to rotate objects after placement. Additionally, I'm now exploring the implementation of a comprehensive undo/redo system. This will track all actions: placement, scaling, moving, rotation, and deletion, giving the user control.


**Implementing Rotation Details**

I've specified the updates needed for object rotation in "VendorSketch Pro". I'm adding a `rotation` property to the data model. The UI will include a rotation handle for dragging and a numeric input for precise control. I'm leaning toward keeping the AABB collision detection, perhaps using the rotated object's bounding box, to maintain the "intuitive and simple" design. I've noted a "rotated rectangle" collision method might be too complex for now.


**Refining Collision Detection**

I'm now thinking about the rotation collision approach. I'll aim for a simplified method that satisfies the user's primary concern - detecting if objects are touching - without needing full physics simulation. I'm focusing on the undo/redo functionality next, with state management as a priority. I'll be exploring the use of history stacks for this purpose. I plan to add Undo and Redo buttons to the UI.


**Finalizing Implementation Steps**

I'm now zeroing in on the implementation. Keyboard shortcuts are a priority, and I'll pass undo/redo handlers to components. I'm focusing on `components/DraggableItem.tsx` and adding the rotation handle logic, along with style and `onPointerMove` updates. I'll add the rotation input and Undo/Redo buttons to the control component. Collision logic updates will also happen, maybe using a simplified rotated bounding box. I plan to use `history` and `redoStack` arrays for undo/redo state management.

payload.text: Make it so when i select an object with th curser, it toggles the custom menu on and off, so that i can make edits

**Refining Toggle Logic**

I'm currently focused on refining the custom menu's toggle behavior. It's now triggered by object selection, as requested. The menu's position in `Controls.tsx` at the bottom remains the same, but the crucial change is the toggle now responds to the cursor's interaction with objects, displaying object details upon selection.


**Analyzing Toggle Functionality**

I'm now investigating the "on/off" toggle behavior. The initial setup shows selection via `selectedId` in `Designer.tsx`, canvas clicks setting `selectedId` to null, and `DraggableItem.tsx` triggering `onSelect`.  I'm now examining the logic to confirm that clicking the same item again deselects it. I see the canvas click handler already has a `null` for `onSelect`. I'm starting to think about how to use that null to toggle.


**Refining Toggle Behavior**

I'm now implementing the toggle logic in `Canvas.tsx`. My goal is to refine the `onSelect` function to deselect an object if it's already selected and select it if it's not. I've considered that the toggle could impact the *Sidebar* visibility, but in most situations, the custom menu refers to the property editor, so that remains the focus. I'm considering potential changes to the position of `Controls.tsx`, specifically moving the custom menu to be closer to the selected object. I will provide a clearer "Edit" prompt.


**Clarifying Menu Visibility**

I'm now focusing on making the custom menu's presence clearer. It should definitely behave as a true "menu". I'm thinking I'll need to update `Designer.tsx` or `Canvas.tsx` to handle the selection toggling. I'll also modify `Controls.tsx` to actually behave like a menu, maybe with an appearance or disappearance animation or other visibility indication. Then, I need to make sure the selected `DraggableItem` feels interactive. Ultimately, the plan is to modify the `setSelectedId` logic or usage in `Designer.tsx`. I am going to see how `Canvas.tsx` can help me with this.


**Implementing Toggle Logic**

I've got the toggle logic in place using the provided code snippet, ensuring that re-selecting an item deselects it. I'm now transitioning the "custom menu" from the footer to a floating "Quick Edit" menu that appears near the selected object. This aligns with the request for an object icon and scalable input. To ensure that it toggles as requested, the property editor is now controlled by the select and deselect events, appearing and disappearing accordingly.


**Enhancing Menu Visibility**

I'm now revising the design for the editing controls. I'm focusing on making the "custom menu" a floating property menu that is far more dynamic. I'm going to modify `Controls.tsx` to handle the show/hide behavior. I will keep the footer for the general actions, while introducing an object-specific editing panel as a floating popover. I'll make the editing panel's display contingent on the active selection state. I'm confident that this approach will deliver a much more intuitive user experience.


**Refining Menu Positioning**

I'm now repositioning the edit menu as a floating element, close to the selected object. The toggle behavior is the primary focus and should activate on selection. I need to make the menu toggle when the cursor interacts with an object, showing the menu on click, and hiding it with another click. The plan now is to modify `Canvas.tsx` for the toggle logic and `Controls.tsx` for the floating menu's rendering condition and animation. I will add a visual indicator for selection in `DraggableItem.tsx`.


**Adjusting Toggle Behavior**

I'm now implementing the toggle logic in `Canvas.tsx`. By modifying `onSelect` with a ternary operator, I can now deselect an object if it's already selected. To make it a true "custom menu", I'm moving the edit inputs to a floating property inspector. This allows it to appear and disappear on object selection. In `Controls.tsx`, I'll only render the inspector when an object is selected. The inspector will now feel like a custom menu.


**Revising Edit Menu**

I'm now overhauling the edit menu for better user interaction. I've decided on a clean slide-up panel for edits, toggled by `selectedId` in `Canvas.tsx`.  I'm moving the floating edit menu to its own component, `FloatingEditMenu`, while `Controls.tsx` will focus on global actions like Export.  This will create the intended floating "input box" that appears with object selection.


**Designing Floating Menu**

I'm now starting to design the floating property menu. I plan to use a new component or update `Controls` for this, positioning it near the selected object or at a fixed, predictable spot like the bottom-right. The specific inputs (Width, Length, Rotation) will be contained within this menu. I also need to make sure the selected object has a clearer highlight for visual feedback, and I'll create a modern, translucent floating card with glassmorphism effects for the "Custom Menu". I plan to update `Canvas.tsx` for the `onSelect` callback, `Controls.tsx` for a floating or reactive footer, and possibly a floating panel in `Designer.tsx` for the inputs and a floating menu directly next to the object based on the latest context.


**Refactoring Edit Components**

I'm now refactoring the edit components for clarity. I'm moving the "Export Design" button to the header to keep the footer clear for object-specific controls. The selection toggle logic is now in `Canvas.tsx`, and I'm updating `Controls.tsx`, possibly renaming it to `ObjectEditMenu`, so it only renders the custom menu when an object is selected. I'll make the editing inputs (Width, Length, Rotation) and other settings available there, but only show it when selected. I'll need to figure out where to show the export button in the header.

payload.text:
**Analyzing the Error**

I've pinpointed a TypeScript error in `components/Canvas.tsx` at line 70, stating "Expected 2 arguments, but got 3." I'm reviewing the `onUpdate` function call. Lines 69-71 are the focus. It seems a mismatch in the argument count within the `onUpdate` event handler call.


**Refining the Interface**

I've clarified the root cause. The `onUpdate` function, passed to `Canvas`, expects a third argument, `finalUpdate`, in Designer, yet the interface declared only two. The fix is to update the interface `CanvasProps` in `components/Canvas.tsx` to match the 3 argument signature of the event handler. I need to make sure the prop signature and the implementation align.


**Confirming the Update**

I've confirmed the fix. The `onUpdate` prop signature in `CanvasProps` needs a third, optional, boolean argument `finalUpdate`. I've verified the `DraggableItem` component correctly passes this argument. Now, the implementation in `Canvas.tsx` at line 70 is where the argument is used. The original error, the type mismatch, is solved by updating the interface signature.

entries.description: Update CanvasProps interface to accept an optional third argument for onUpdate to match its usage and the implementation in Designer.tsx.

I'm currently focused on improving the visual presentation of "VendorSketch Pro." Specifically, I'm exploring how to introduce padding around the drawing area to mimic a site map effect, preventing the design from appearing flush against the UI edges. Additionally, I'm considering methods for easily drawing a snaking queue within the application. I'll need to explore different implementations and visual representations for queue drawings.


**Developing Queue Implementation**

I've progressed to implementing the "Collection Point" and "Service Point" objects, updating `types.ts` and `OBJECT_DEFAULTS` accordingly. The focus is now on the "Queue Link." I've decided to prioritize a "Queue Segment" object that users can place and rotate, aligning with the existing object selection pattern. This approach emphasizes intuitive placement rather than a complex path-drawing tool. Updating the `CATEGORIES` section is next, along with the canvas padding and Meters Legend enhancements.


**Refining Queue Elements**

I'm now refining the "Queue Segment" object. Its dimensions are set to 2m x 0.1m, serving as a modular "Queue Barrier." I'll also add "Service Point" (2m x 0.6m) and "Collection Point" (1m x 0.6m) to `types.ts` and the UI. Additionally, I'll update the `Sidebar.tsx` to include new categories and items. The next step will involve canvas padding in `Canvas.tsx`, and maybe a revisit to collision detection for the queue barrier in `utils/collisions.ts`.

payload.text:
Please make it so when you click on an object that all the editable options stay open.

Make the space outside of the boundary 3x as large
Also add the following objects:
Fire extinguishers, entry/exit point

*Without editing the code, how difficult would it be to make a stand plan collaboratable on two devices at the same time? Its very important to not affect individuals who are working at the same time on different projects on the site*

**Refining Selection Behavior**

I'm currently focused on improving the interaction with the food stand sketching app. I'm prioritizing the Custom Menu. My goal is for editable options to remain open when an object is selected. That means the focus is no longer to toggle, but to display and retain user-selected customizations.


**Improving Object Persistence**

I've realized the existing toggle functionality in `Canvas.tsx` needs adjustment; I'll change the `onSelect` implementation to always select. Also, I'm thinking of increasing the `paddingMeters` to a wider boundary, maybe 15m. I'm expanding `ObjectType` and `OBJECT_DEFAULTS` to include 'Fire Extinguisher' and 'Entry/Exit Point', which I'll also add to `Sidebar.tsx`. I also need to make sure to respond about collaborative work.

To make a stand plan collaboratable in real-time across multiple devices (like Google Docs or Figma), you would need to implement a **real-time backend sync layer**. 

Technically, this is straightforward for a senior engineer but requires:
1.  **State Synchronization**: Moving the local `objects` and `boundary` state to a real-time database (like **Firebase Realtime DB**, **Supabase**, or **PocketBase**).
2.  **Conflict Resolution**: Using a library like **Yjs** or **Automerge** (CRDTs) to handle the case where two people move the same object at the exact same millisecond.
3.  **Project Isolation**: Each stand would need a unique URL or ID (e.g., `sketcher.app/project/123`). This ensures that traders working on "Burger Stand A" never see or affect the data for "Taco Truck B".

Without changing the current code structure, adding this would involve replacing the local `useState` for objects with a "live" hook that listens to a database.

Here are the requested updates for the stand plan:

(i) **Canvas**: Increased the "Site Area" padding from 5m to 15m.
(ii) **Selection Behavior**: Modified the click handler so selecting an object always keeps the menu open rather than toggling it off on a second click.
(iii) **New Objects**: Added "Fire Extinguisher" (Red) and "Entry/Exit Point" (High-vis green/white) to the library.
(iv) **Sidebar**: Reorganized categories to include the new safety and access items.

I'm currently focused on defining the new objects and their initial properties. I've specified a "Tent" object with default dimensions and collision behavior. I'm also considering a "Bench Set" object, with initial dimensions. I'm still figuring out how to handle the other requirements.


**Adding New UI Elements**

I'm now implementing the mandatory "Important Information" page. I've defined its trigger (post-login), content (specific points and a declaration), and UI components (title, description, and a required tick box). I'm refactoring the state flow within the application, and will create a new dedicated component for the info page, likely in `components/InfoPage.tsx`. I'm also planning to add the North/South designations on the canvas. Additionally, I'm updating `types.ts` and renaming `Pergoda` to `Pagoda`.

payload.text: Please add a pop up following a successful PNG download when using the export button that says: "Download Successful. Please email the floor plan to dan@savourfestival.com."

**Implementing Success Notification**

I'm currently focused on implementing a success pop-up after the PNG download. My current task is to modify the `components/Designer.tsx` file. To do this, I plan on adding a new state variable called `showSuccessModal`, which will be responsible for tracking the visibility of the pop-up notification.


**Adding Success Modal Logic**

I've added the logic to trigger the success pop-up after the PNG download in `exportDesign`. Now, I'm integrating the modal component within `components/Designer.tsx` to display the success message and include a close button, as per the specifications. I am also making sure that the existing requests, including new objects and labels, are accounted for as well.


**Refining Modal Design**

I'm now refining the success modal's aesthetics to fit the emerald theme of the app, focusing on a clean and intuitive layout. I've re-reviewed the `Designer.tsx` content, confirming the need to integrate the success notification there. I will incorporate the `showSuccessModal` state variable within the component, with logic to trigger the modal after a successful download. I'll also add the necessary JSX for the modal.


**Finalizing Modal Implementation**

I've finished integrating the success modal within `Designer.tsx`, incorporating the `showSuccessModal` state and associated JSX. The modal appears after a successful download, displaying a success message with an email link and a visual confirmation. I've also set up error handling in case the process fails. The final touch will be to add a "close" button.
